# Questions in Livestream 229

Live Stream #229: A Levite on Every Street Corner

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=732" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:12</a> | Is Sloppy based on the scumbag youth pastor? | No; he says Sloppy is not based on that person. | He answers directly that Sloppy is not based on the youth pastor, then avoids further explanation by telling viewers that understanding Sloppy requires reading the books. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=756" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:36</a> | Which of the two novels is better for readers who have read both? | He does not rank them and leaves that judgment to readers. | Rather than choosing a favorite, he says he will leave the comparison to the chat and to readers who have read both books. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:03</a> | What is meant by the line about donkey carts, unfinished high-rises, and corruption, and wasn't ancient Egypt less like modern Egypt? | He says no; in the respects raised, he sees strong continuity between ancient and modern Egypt. | He reads the passage from the book and says the quoted details are meant as Cairo continuity: pyramids remain, donkey carts are still visible, garbage and corruption are persistent features, and ancient Egypt was not fundamentally unlike modern Egypt in those respects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=1404" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:24</a> | Is Egypt in the time of the book still a military dictatorship? | He does not address it directly in the book, but assumes so because he sees that as a long-running pattern in Egypt. | He says the book does not spell this out, but his assumption is yes because Egypt has repeatedly operated as a military dictatorship from the pharaohs through later periods and into the present. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=1463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:23</a> | Will Cairo change much if Egypt finishes its new capital city? | He expects easier navigation and administrative change, not a deep change in Egypt's underlying outlook. | He frames the new capital as a planned administrative and symbolic move near major mosques, not a change in national outlook. The practical advantage he emphasizes is that a new capital would be easier to navigate than old Cairo. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=1614" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:54</a> | Are donkeys cheaper to maintain than trucks? | Yes; he says they are cheaper and remain practical even though trucks are also used. | He says trucks are used, but donkeys remain cheaper and practical for poorer transport. He illustrates this with comments about roadside repairs, livestock transport, and the difference between poorer donkey use and wealthier truck use. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=1801" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:01</a> | Has he tried driving a car in Cairo? | No; he says he would not try it and recommends cabs, buses, or private drivers instead. | He says he has never driven in Cairo and would not attempt it because rental driving is difficult and risky. In his view, cabs, buses, and private drivers are easier and cheaper. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=1867" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:07</a> | If God tabernacles with believers, why would the real presence in communion matter? | He rejects real presence and says Christ is present with gathered believers, so the imperative is to do communion in remembrance of Christ. | He distinguishes "within" from "with us" and states his in-communial view: Christ is present where believers gather, so he does not need to be present in the bread or elements. He also notes that Catholics, Orthodox, and some Protestants disagree with him on real presence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=2226" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:06</a> | Does orthopraxy supersede orthodoxy with communion? | Yes; he says communion is one place where Christ gives an imperative to do something, so right practice is central. | He says communion is a case where Christ commands an action: "do this." Because orthopraxy is right practice and orthodoxy is right belief, he treats communion as primarily an imperative to act rightly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=2306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:26</a> | If Israelites did not practice parallel polygamy in Egypt, how could Deuteronomy 21 assume two wives, and how could that be afforded? | He says parallel polygamy was encountered in Sinai and the Levant, not brought from Egypt, and wealth in flocks made it possible for some men. | He distinguishes serial polygamy, which he says Egyptians practiced, from parallel polygamy, which Egypt allowed only for the king. Deuteronomy addresses a practice Israelites encountered after leaving Egypt among Sinai and Levantine groups, and he says wealth in flocks and herd labor explains how some men could afford it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=2810" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:50</a> | If polygamy existed before Torah, was Yahweh fine with it? | No; he says the texts can describe a practice without endorsing it. | He says polygamy was not new with the Midianites or Abraham, but that does not mean God approved it. His distinction is that biblical description is not the same as prescription. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=2917" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:37</a> | How can someone better understand the Christian calendar versus the Hebrew calendar? | He recommends using actual calendars; Hebrew reckoning is lunar, while Christian practice follows Julian or Gregorian traditions depending on communion. | He explains the Hebrew calendar as a lunar calendar, now usually calculated mathematically rather than by personal moon observation, and recommends buying a Hebrew calendar. For Christian calendars, he distinguishes Julian and Gregorian traditions and notes that some Orthodox groups still use Julian reckoning converted into Gregorian dates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=3186" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:06</a> | Are binging astronomy videos, using Universe Sandbox and Space Engine, and browsing sky resources too much? | He says he has used Universe Sandbox for research and finds it useful, while also stressing that writers should read broadly. | He treats the tools as useful rather than excessive, saying he has used Universe Sandbox for research. At the same time, he cautions that writers should build a broad knowledge base instead of relying on one type of resource. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=3506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:26</a> | How can a writer learn hard sci-fi material without being a scientist or leaning on AI? | He recommends reading large amounts of nonfiction and introductory textbooks instead of relying on AI. | His advice is to read widely and use accessible nonfiction, textbooks, and introductory material to build enough competence for fiction. He presents this as better preparation than asking AI to supply the science. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=3681" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:21</a> | How should writers approach publishing? | He says nonfiction may still fit traditional publishing, fiction often pushes writers toward independent publishing, and either way good beta readers and patience matter. | He separates nonfiction from fiction: nonfiction can still make sense for traditional publishers, while fiction writers may find independent publishing more realistic. He stresses revision, beta readers, and patience rather than rushing a manuscript out. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=3997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:37</a> | What is a developmental editor? | A developmental editor works on plot, structure, and large-scale reshaping of the book. | He explains that a developmental editor works at the big-picture level, reading for story, structure, plot, and major changes rather than copyediting small wording issues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=4240" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:40</a> | What are heavenly or spiritual gifts, and how should they be used without abuse? | He treats them as Spirit-given abilities for the good of the church and says abusing them includes turning them into a personal revenue stream. | He describes spiritual gifts as gifts of the Spirit intended to serve the church. Abuse happens when someone turns them into self-promotion, manipulation, or a way to make money from other believers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=4396" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:16</a> | How can someone remain Christian after learning ancient Egypt extensively? | He says he sees no conflict between Egyptology and Christian faith. | He answers from personal experience: extensive study of ancient Egypt has not made Christianity untenable for him, and he does not see the field itself as a reason to abandon the faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=4446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:06</a> | Would the Holy Spirit refuse to act if spiritual gifts were used for personal gain? | He says people do misuse gifts for personal gain, though he expects consequences and does not rule out the gifts being withdrawn. | He says misuse of gifts does happen and may bring consequences. He does not state a mechanical rule that the Spirit always refuses to act, but he leaves open that a gift could be withdrawn. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=4613" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:53</a> | Conscious of what community? | He describes the "conscious community" as a modern remaking of Egyptian religion for an African-centered identity. | He explains the term as a movement that reworks Egyptian religion and imagery into a modern African-centered identity, rather than as straightforward ancient Egyptian religion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=4699" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:19</a> | Is Kara Cooney into conscious-community material? | No; he says she is an atheist and not part of that movement, even if she has appeared on some related channels. | He says Kara Cooney is not part of the conscious community and identifies her instead as an atheist scholar. He notes that appearing on related channels does not mean she belongs to that movement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=4766" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:26</a> | What is your opinion of the Voynich manuscript and whether it should be decoded? | He thinks it is probably a fake or prank artifact rather than a meaningful text. | He says he doubts the manuscript encodes a real recoverable text and leans toward treating it as a fake or prank. Because of that, he is skeptical that "decoding" it will produce meaningful content. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=4878" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:18</a> | When studying ancient Egyptian, did you need enough fluency to write full conversations in hieroglyphs? | He says he did not need that level for coursework, but he once had it and wrote journal entries in Egyptian. | He says coursework did not require conversational fluency in hieroglyphs. Still, at one point he had enough command of Egyptian to write personal journal entries in it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=5038" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:58</a> | How effective was the khopesh as a short-range battlefield weapon? | He says it was a last-resort weapon; Egyptian soldiers would rather have spear or bow. | He describes the khopesh as a backup or last-resort weapon rather than a preferred battlefield tool. In his account, an Egyptian soldier would rather fight with spear or bow than close in with a khopesh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=5254" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:34</a> | Was the khopesh made for style points? | He is not sure, but says it did not give a major battlefield advantage compared with a spear. | He does not claim certainty about why the weapon had its shape, but he emphasizes that it was short and did not outperform a spear in ordinary battlefield use. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=5342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:02</a> | Was Ramesses II viewed as a god during his lifetime, not only after death? | Yes; he says that practice ran from Amenhotep III through Ramesses XI. | He says New Kingdom kings in this line were treated as divine while alive, not only after death. He places the practice from Amenhotep III through Ramesses XI. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=5649" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:09</a> | Why did Egyptians wage war against the Nubians? | Primarily for Nubian gold, especially for funerary and afterlife uses. | He identifies Nubian gold as the central motive. Egypt wanted actual gold for the body, tomb, and funerary equipment because it mattered materially in Egyptian afterlife practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=5794" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:34</a> | Could Egyptians just give a voice offering for gold instead of using actual gold? | No; he says voice offerings could be made to gods, but the deceased still needed actual metal for the body and funerary goods. | He distinguishes a spoken or voice offering from the physical requirements of the dead. In his explanation, verbal offerings did not replace the need for actual gold on or with the body. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=5967" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:27</a> | Did Egyptians make any gold weapons? | Yes, rarely; he cites a gold dagger from Tutankhamun's tomb that was unusually hard for a gold blade. | He says gold weapons were rare and uses Tutankhamun's gold dagger as the example. He notes that it was unusually hard for a gold blade, which leads into the alloy discussion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=6028" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:28</a> | Was that gold weapon alloyed? | Yes; he says it was alloyed, roughly around 18 karat as he remembers it. | He answers that the dagger was not pure soft gold. From memory, he describes it as alloyed and roughly around 18 karat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=6062" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:02</a> | Was the khopesh more a knife than a sword? | He says yes, given that its blade was only about thirteen inches long. | He agrees with the knife comparison because the blade length he gives is about thirteen inches. That short length is part of why he treats it as a close-range backup rather than a primary battlefield sword. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=6088" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:28</a> | What was the alloy metal in Tutankhamun's gold dagger? | He says the exact composition is not known from the surface tests he remembers, only that it appears to be about 18 karat. | He says the testing he remembers was surface-level and did not identify a precise alloying metal. The cautious conclusion he gives is only that the dagger appears to be about 18 karat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=6182" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:02</a> | Did Egyptians care if Nubians borrowed or syncretized Egyptian gods? | No; he says Egyptians were not especially possessive about others adopting their gods. | He says Egyptians did not object strongly to Nubians taking up or adapting Egyptian deities. In his framing, Egyptian religion could be exported or adopted without Egyptians treating that as a problem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=6482" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:02</a> | Does the name Bethuel mean "daughter of El"? | He says probably not and treats the etymology as uncertain. | He works through the Hebrew rather than giving a firm folk etymology, and he does not accept "daughter of El" as the likely meaning. His answer remains cautious because the name's derivation is uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=6627" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:27</a> | What sources is he using to look up etymologies of names? | In the moment he is mainly looking at the Hebrew, but says fuller work would need HALOT, Amorite, and comparative West Semitic sources. | He says his immediate check is based on the Hebrew form, but a serious etymology would need reference tools and comparative material such as HALOT, Amorite evidence, and broader West Semitic parallels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=6777" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:57</a> | What do you recommend for reading on Israelite slavery? | He points to Richard Averbeck as a starting place, says new work keeps appearing, and advises against using Josh Bowen as the starting point. | He recommends starting with Richard Averbeck and notes that scholarship on the topic continues to develop. He warns that Josh Bowen should not be the initial guide for this subject. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=6921" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:21</a> | Could Levites claim the ancestry of the tribe they lived among, given the Heman and Ethan passages? | No; he says the temple singer Heman is not the older Judahite Heman, and Levites did not simply inherit another tribe's lineage. | He argues that the Heman in the temple singer context should not be identified with the earlier Judahite Heman. On that basis, he rejects using residence among a tribe as proof that Levites could claim that tribe's ancestry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=7588" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:28</a> | Do different manuscript families have superscriptions in the Psalms? | He says the main witnesses are the Masoretic Text and Septuagint, whose superscriptions differ but likely preserve the same core attribution. | He says the relevant comparison is mainly between the Masoretic Text and the Septuagint. Their Psalm superscriptions are not identical, but he treats them as likely preserving a shared core attribution in many cases. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=7735" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:55</a> | How should someone handle toxic co-workers? | He says it depends on the workplace and behavior, but the basic rule is not to sink to their level. | He says the response depends on the kind of workplace and the behavior involved. His baseline advice is to avoid retaliating in kind or letting toxic co-workers pull you down to their behavior. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=7878" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:18</a> | How can the Bible's Son of God not be the same as Pharaoh as son of Ra? | He calls that a false equivalence based on surface similarity rather than identity. | He says the reasoning confuses two uses of son-language as if a shared phrase made the concepts identical. In his view, Pharaoh as son of Ra and biblical Son of God language operate in different contexts and cannot simply be equated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=8111" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:11</a> | Is that the fallacy of the undistributed middle? | Yes; he agrees that the false-equivalence reasoning fits that fallacy. | He agrees that the argument has the structure of an undistributed-middle fallacy: two things share one predicate, but that does not prove they are the same thing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=8186" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:26</a> | Did Baal ever evolve into a moon god, or was Hubal an imported Baal idol? | He says Baal was not a moon deity and does not think the Hubal claim is established from the evidence he knows. | He rejects the idea that Baal became a moon god and treats the Hubal connection as unproven. He frames the claim as speculative rather than something supported by evidence he knows. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=8453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:53</a> | Was the stream title meant as a reference to Black Hebrew Israelites? | No; he says that was not the intention. | He says the title was not intended as a Black Hebrew Israelite reference. The answer is brief and direct, with no broader claim attached. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=8515" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:55</a> | Can you recommend books for New Testament historical and cultural background? | He says that is not his area and points viewers to Carmen Joy Imes's reading lists instead. | He declines to present himself as the right source for New Testament background recommendations. Instead, he directs viewers toward Carmen Joy Imes's lists as a better place to start. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=8764" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:04</a> | What do you think of pronouncing the divine name as Yahu in Josephus? | He rejects that reading and says older Semitic evidence points more toward Yahweh or Yahweh-like pronunciation. | He argues that Josephus should be read in light of his time and transmission context, not used alone to overturn older Semitic evidence. He thinks the evidence favors Yahweh or something Yahweh-like rather than "Yahu" as the full pronunciation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=9068" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:08</a> | Why not pronounce the divine name as Yave? | He says the ancient transcriptions tend to favor a W sound over a V sound, though the evidence is not absolutely conclusive. | He says the issue turns partly on how the ancient consonant is represented, and he thinks the evidence leans toward W rather than V. He still presents this as a best-supported reconstruction, not absolute certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=9417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:57</a> | What kinds of mythical creatures did ancient Egypt have? | He says Egypt had many desert and underworld creatures, including flying snakes and numerous demon forms. | He describes Egyptian mythic creatures as especially common in desert and underworld settings. Examples include flying snakes and many demon-like forms rather than a single neat monster category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=9610" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:10</a> | Were jinn a thing in ancient Egypt, even if not in the Islamic sense? | Not exactly; he says Egyptians did believe in spirit beings, but not jinn in the later Islamic sense. | He says Egyptians had spirit beings and hostile supernatural entities, but "jinn" is the wrong category if it means the later Islamic concept. The closest comparison is general spirit belief, not a direct match. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=9631" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:31</a> | Has he seen Turk's most recent Exodus video? | Yes, but he has not responded because he says it did not engage his objections. | He says he watched the video but sees no reason to respond because, in his view, it does not deal with the objections he had already raised. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=9684" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:24</a> | Are there snakes that can jump or glide? | Yes; he mentions gliding snakes in Southeast Asia, but says none are known from the ancient Near East. | He acknowledges that some snakes can glide, especially in Southeast Asia. He separates that from the ancient Near Eastern context, where he says no such snakes are known. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=9835" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:55</a> | Did Egypt have any "good" mythical creatures like unicorns? | He says unicorn material is better attested in Assyrian contexts than Egyptian ones. | He does not identify unicorns as an Egyptian feature. Instead, he says one-horned or unicorn-like material is better associated with Assyrian contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=9888" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:48</a> | How did unicorns develop as an idea? | He says people could create one-horned sheep or goats by manipulating horn growth, and that this was reproduced in modern experiments. | He explains unicorn development partly through animal husbandry: if horns are manipulated early, sheep or goats can grow a single horn. He says this has been reproduced experimentally, which makes such reports plausible without requiring a separate species. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=10090" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:10</a> | Can you do a popcorn review of *The Ten Commandments*? | He says no, not on YouTube, because content-ID and copyright issues block that format. | He says the problem is not lack of interest but platform restrictions. A movie-review format using clips would run into content ID and copyright problems on YouTube. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=10310" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:50</a> | What is the best way to root sin out of your life? | He points to spiritual disciplines: prayer, Bible reading, biblical meditation, communion, and participation in the church. | He answers with ordinary Christian disciplines rather than a quick trick: prayer, reading Scripture, meditating on it biblically, taking communion, and remaining involved in the church. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=10682" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:02</a> | Has he tried Rumble for movie review content? | Yes; he says Rumble has similar content rules to YouTube and pays less. | He says Rumble does not solve the copyright-review problem because its rules are similar, and he adds that the platform pays less than YouTube. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=10900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:40</a> | Have you seen Professor Laurel Bestock's Wired video, and what is your take? | He thinks it is mostly good, with a few minor disagreements. | He says he has seen the video and regards it as mostly good. His disagreements are minor rather than objections to the whole presentation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=10957" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:37</a> | Why was Samson granted super strength for a Nazirite vow? | He says the text does not fully explain it, but Samson functioned as a last-ditch judge against the Philistines. | He says the biblical text does not give a full causal explanation for the strength. He interprets Samson's role functionally as a desperate or last-ditch judge raised up against the Philistines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=11116" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:16</a> | Should Christians give money to the state of Israel for religious reasons? | He says Scripture does not require that and cautions that donors should know exactly where money is going. | He says Christians are not scripturally obligated to donate to the state of Israel for religious reasons. If someone gives anyway, he warns them to know where the money goes and not assume it is automatically righteous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=11217" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:57</a> | Could David and Bathsheba actually have been stoned for adultery? | He says no in practice, because David would have had to judge himself. | He says the legal issue is complicated by David's kingship: practically, David would have been the one positioned to judge the case, so the punishment could not realistically be applied against him in the ordinary way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=11253" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:33</a> | What is the earliest attestation for Sea Peoples or Philistines settling Canaan in a late-Exodus framework? | He places Philistines on the coastal plain by early Ramesses II and notes earlier Sea Peoples references in the Amarna period. | He says Philistines are on the coastal plain by early Ramesses II in the framework he is using. He also notes that Sea Peoples are attested earlier in Amarna-period references, so the phenomenon is not limited to the later famous invasions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=11621" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:41</a> | Is redirecting charity money laundering, embezzlement, or another crime? | He says not necessarily, especially with foreign charities, which may not be under the same receipt and disclosure expectations. | He distinguishes unethical-sounding redirection from specific legal categories. In foreign-charity contexts especially, he says receipt and reporting expectations may differ, so the answer is not automatically money laundering or embezzlement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=12117" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:57</a> | On Jericho's Late Bronze destruction, will Nigro say who caused it or avoid the controversy? | He expects Nigro to avoid making the attribution explicit. | He says Nigro tends to avoid controversy and is already in enough academic tension over the dating. He expects him to state the Late Bronze 2B destruction evidence and leave the audience to infer implications rather than saying the Israelites did it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=12274" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:34</a> | What is your view of the Midianite hypothesis? | He says he broadly holds it to be probably correct. | He defines the Midianite hypothesis as Moses encountering Yahweh in Midian and bringing that knowledge back to Israelites in Egypt. He accepts the basic form while warning that claims about Yahweh as a volcano, mountain, mining, or smithing deity go beyond what is established. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=12515" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:35</a> | At what point did Edom become its own territory, and were its borders fixed? | He says Edom emerges as its own zone by the Iron Age, but the borders stayed fluid. | He says Egyptian evidence points to Edom as a recognizable territory around the Ramesside era, with a zone running from the southern Dead Sea area toward the Gulf of Aqaba. The borders were not fixed modern borders and shifted where Edom met Moab, Judah, and other groups. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=12663" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:03</a> | Are Ammon, Moab, and Edom just adjacent territories without fixed borders like modern states? | Broadly yes; he says those borders shifted, though rivers like the Arnon and Jabbok could mark boundaries at different times. | He agrees that the territories were not stable modern states with permanent borders. Rivers such as the Arnon and Jabbok could serve as boundaries at certain times, but expansion and contraction changed the lines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=12771" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:51</a> | Were the Midianites known for activity at Timna, and does that support the hypothesis under discussion? | He says Timna does connect them with the region, though broader conclusions remain speculative. | He says Midianites were associated with mining and smelting activity at Timna, especially copper. That connection is why some link the hypothesis to mining or smithing, but he stresses that this further step remains speculative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=12854" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:14</a> | What do you think of the idea that Midianites could have understood Yahweh in a different light? | He treats that kind of claim as speculative rather than settled. | He says some Midianites could be connected with Abrahamic descent lines, such as Ishmaelites, and he does not object to that in principle. But he treats claims about how they understood Yahweh as conjectural, especially given later population shifts in Arabia and Midian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=13373" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:53</a> | What do you think about conlangs, and do you have favorites? | He likes them for worldbuilding, but says they should be used sparingly in fiction. | He defines conlangs as constructed languages and gives examples such as Klingon and Tolkien's languages. He likes them, but says foreign-language material can make readers tune out, so fiction should use it sparingly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=13632" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:12</a> | Is the ancient speech in his book an Egyptian-based conlang? | Yes; he says the ancient speech is an Egyptian-based constructed language. | He confirms that the ancient speech is an Egyptian-based conlang. He says conlangs are useful worldbuilding, but he does not make the whole novel revolve around explaining them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=13852" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:52</a> | Does he use the International Phonetic Alphabet? | No; he says he does not get into linguistics that deeply. | He says he does not use IPA and does not go that deeply into linguistics for this work, even though he is comfortable using language material for worldbuilding. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/QmWPNaKetvE?t=14082" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:42</a> | Can Eusebius's history be trusted, and why was he caught up in the Arian dispute? | He says Eusebius must be used critically because friendship and Arian sympathies created bias, though some of his history is still useful. | He answers "yes and no": Eusebius preserves useful history, but his closeness to Arius and the still-unsettled Nicene dispute affected his judgment. He says Eusebius later came around after Arius was anathematized, but his biases mean readers must use him critically. |
